title: Gravitationally Induced Quantum Decoherence of Macroscopic Objects
authors: Hiroki Takeda, Shogo Tomizuka, Takahiro Tanaka
abstract: We formulate the gravitationally induced quantum decoherence of a massive object prepared in a spatial superposition. Starting from linearized gravity coupled to a massive system particle and an environmental scalar field, we derive a closed-time-path influence functional governing the reduced system dynamics. In the nonrelativistic and quasi-static regime, the decoherence exponent can be written as a bilinear functional of the difference of the system stress-energy tensors and an effective noise kernel obtained by dressing the environmental stress-energy tensor correlator with graviton propagators. We then apply this framework to the Newtonian long-range gravitational interaction and evaluate the resulting decoherence function for a dilute nonrelativistic gas modeled by finite wave packets and coarse-grained in time and space. By performing controlled approximations, we obtain analytic expressions for the cumulative decoherence function and show that the dominant contribution is accumulated logarithmically over a broad range of distances, while remaining subdominant to conventional collisional decoherence under realistic conditions.
summary: This theoretical work calculates the decoherence rate for macroscopic quantum superpositions induced by gravitational coupling to an environment. Using the closed-time-path formalism, the authors find that while a logarithmic dependence on distance exists, the gravitational decoherence is generally negligible compared to standard gas collisions. This sets a quantitative limit on observing gravity-induced decoherence in current experimental setups.
main_problem: The paper investigates the quantum decoherence of massive objects when subjected to gravitational interactions with an environment.
main_result: The calculated gravitational decoherence function is found to be subdominant compared to conventional collisional decoherence under realistic, dilute gas conditions.
method: The authors use the closed-time-path influence functional formalism to derive the reduced system dynamics, integrating out environmental degrees of freedom.
model_or_system: The model involves a massive system particle prepared in a spatial superposition, coupled to linearized gravity and an environmental scalar field, specialized for a dilute nonrelativistic gas.
key_observables: Decoherence exponent/function ($\Gamma$), reduced density matrix ($ho_r$), and the environmental noise kernel.
important_parameters: Mass ($M$), separation distance ($\Delta x$), gas temperature ($T_{	ext{gas}}$), and various momentum/length scales ($k_{	ext{UV}}, k_{\sigma}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis is performed in the nonrelativistic and quasi-static regime, utilizing approximations like the dipole-like parametrization for the system source.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows the dependence of $\log_{10} \Gamma$ on mass and separation distance, while Figure 3 compares the ratio of gravitational to collisional decoherence.
paper_structure: The paper systematically derives the influence functional, models the environmental kernel using windowing/convolution for physical regularization, and finally calculates the cumulative decoherence function by separating diagonal and off-diagonal contributions.
why_interesting: It provides a first-principles, quantitative benchmark for estimating decoherence rates from fundamental forces (gravity) in macroscopic quantum systems, which is crucial for testing quantum gravity effects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Contour plot of log10 Γ in the (∆x, M) plane, obtained by numerically evaluating the saturated limit r →∞of Eq. (4.24) for a dilute thermal gas with m = 28 amu ≃4.65×10−26 kg, Tgas = 300 K, Pgas = 1 atm ≃1.0×105 Pa. Dashed lines indicate constant values of log10 Γ, and shaded regions show its magnitude. Representative experimental regimes are overlaid: matter-wave interferometers, levitated particles, and optomechanical systems. The system size is fixed to Rs = 10−7 m, and the resulting dependence on Rs is weak.

FIG. 3. Contour plots of log10(Γ/Γcol) in the (Rs, M) plane for fixed superposition sizes: (a) ∆x = 10−13 m, (b) ∆x = 10−8 m, and (c) ∆x = 10−3 m. Here Γ is obtained by numerically evaluating the saturated limit r →∞of Eq. (4.24), while Γcol is estimated from Eq. (4.47) for a dilute thermal gas with m = 28 amu ≃4.65 × 10−26 kg, Tgas = 300 K, and Pgas = 1 atm ≃ 1.0×105 Pa. Dashed lines indicate constant values of log10(Γ/Γcol). In each panel, only the representative experimental regime relevant to the chosen value of ∆x is overlaid schematically.
